Camels are able to eat cactuses because of specialized adaptations in their mouths.
The inside of a camelâs mouth is covered with thick, leathery tissue and cone-shaped papillae that protect it from sharp spines. When chewing, saliva helps soften the cactus spines, allowing the camel to swallow without injury.
This adaptation enables camels to survive in arid environments where few food sources are availableâdemonstrating how evolution supports survival in extreme conditions.

They said he would never think, never communicate, and never live a meaningful life. His father, Dick Hoyt, refused to accept that as the final word.

Years later, when Rick was a teenager, his parents discovered that his mind was fully alert. Using a special communication device, Rick was finally able to express himself. One day, he typed a simple sentence that changed everything: he wanted to participate in a charity run to support a fellow student. Dick, who was not an athlete and had no training, said yes. He pushed Rick in a wheelchair through the race and finished it.

That one decision became a lifetime commitment. Dick realized that when Rick was racing, he felt free. From that day on, Dick pushed, pulled, and carried his son through marathons, triathlons, Ironman races, and even the Boston Marathon often qualifying with times that elite runners struggle to achieve. Dick ran while pushing Rick in a wheelchair, swam while towing him in a raft, and cycled with Rick in a custom seat attached to his bike. Together, they completed over 1,000 endurance events.

Rick once said that when he wasnât racing, he felt disabled but when he was racing with his father, he felt like everyone else. Dick didnât see himself as a hero. He said he was simply doing what any parent should do: listening to his child and showing up every day. Even as Dick aged and his body weakened, he kept going because stopping meant taking freedom away from his son. They showed the world that limitations donât define a life, love does. And sometimes, the strongest force in the world isnât muscle or speed, but a parent who refuses to let their child be left behind.

We often take our health for granted and forget to appreciate it in our daily lives.

When a 15-year-old steps onto a stage and delivers a performance powerful enough to silence a room, you know something special is happening. Oda Jonetta Jobergâs rendition of Siaâs âHeliumâ on The Voice Norway wasnât just impressive for her age â it was emotionally devastating in the best way. From the first note, she approached the song with restraint, maturity, and an understanding far beyond her years, allowing the lyrics to breathe and the emotion to build naturally.

âHeliumâ is a song that demands vulnerability. Itâs about love, dependence, and the quiet fear of falling without support â and Oda captured that tension perfectly. Instead of overpowering the song, she leaned into its fragility, balancing control with raw feeling. Her vocal choices were intentional, her tone steady yet aching, creating a performance that felt lived-in rather than rehearsed. It wasnât about showing off range â it was about telling the story honestly.

When people look into your eyes, they should feel a story.

They should see a story in your eyes.

Most people run from the bad times, the rock bottom, the struggle, but that time is what will make you into the person you need to be.

When you skip that time, you will never reach your full potential.

So learn to smile in the rain.

And if you are in the cold, learn to dance in the cold.

Learn to dance in the rain.

Learn to smile through the heat and let it shape you like a motherfucking diamond.
